title: FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure - Mexico Business News
snippet: FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure  Mexico Business News
content:
FIFA World Cup 2026 to Challenge Mexico’s Digital Infrastructure
Less than one year before the FIFA World Cup 2026 begins, Mexico faces the challenge of upgrading its digital infrastructure to meet the stringent technological standards set by FIFA. The country shows a measurable lag in connectivity and spectrum availability compared to its co-hosts, the United States and Canada, requiring accelerated execution of public-private strategies to ensure the event's operational viability.
The push for this technological modernization stems not from a domestic government initiative, but from a direct requirement imposed by FIFA. According to Norberto Maldonado, Managing Partner, Sellcom Solutions, the technology upgrades are a FIFA requirement, not a request by the Mexican government.
The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be unprecedented in scale and complexity. It will be the first edition with 48 teams and 104 matches in three host countries, which significantly increases the demand for technological resources compared to previous tournaments. FIFA's requirements aim to standardize a high-quality experience for media, teams, and global audiences.
Previous tournaments established a clear precedent. In Russia 2018, the focus was on robust 4G/LTE networks and the introduction of video assistant referee (VAR) systems with 4K broadcasts. In Qatar 2022, the leap was the widespread deployment of 5G networks in stadiums and high-density areas by operators such as Ericsson and Ooredoo. This enabled gigabit speeds and managed unprecedented data traffic spikes. During the semifinal between France and Morocco, data exchanges exceeded 132TB in a single minute, a milestone demonstrating the critical importance of telecommunications infrastructure.
For 2026, this level of demand is expected not only to be matched but significantly surpassed. Mexico, which will host the opening match at Azteca Stadium, now Banorte Stadium, and additional matches in Guadalajara and Monterrey, must ensure its infrastructure can support this traffic volume and offer advanced digital services.
Current Infrastructure Assessment
Mexico's main obstacle is the telecommunications infrastructure gap compared to its North American partners. Data from Ookla shows that Mexico's average fixed broadband download speed is 90Mbps, far below the United States with 291Mbps and Canada with 243Mbps. The disparity is more pronounced in mobile broadband, where Mexico averages 48Mbps, compared to 176Mbps in the United States and 105Mbps in Canada.
Compounding this issue is a historical delay in radio spectrum allocation, essential for 5G deployment. Temporary licenses have been granted, such as an experimental concession to Televisión Azteca in the 600MHz band for 8K test transmissions. However, these are stopgap measures and do not address the structural deficit.
Various entities have launched initiatives to close the gap, reports Proceso. In January 2025, the Federal Telecommunications Institute (IFT) approved the Federal Electricity Commission’s (CFE) acquisition of a stake in Altán Redes to reactivate the Red Compartida, a wholesale 4G infrastructure network. The IFT also established a 5G technical committee to coordinate and assess the rollout of this technology.
Efforts are also underway to expand high-capacity fiber optic links through alliances such as CFE-Telmex. Expansion of internet node networks and data centers is also in progress. In the private sector, sponsors such as Visa are promoting digital payments to make stadiums cashless environments, equipping venues, public transportation, and nearby businesses with terminals and mobile app support.
Finally, emerging technologies are expected to enhance the event experience. Maldonado highlights the introduction of an AI-assisted VAR system, where an algorithm will analyze plays to reduce human error. Stadiums in Mexico, particularly the Azteca Stadium, are expected to offer immersive experiences using virtual reality devices, allowing spectators to view matches from premium perspectives in real time. Security will also incorporate AI, with facial and behavioral monitoring systems for access control and risk detection.
These infrastructure investments could be turned into permanent assets that accelerate Mexico’s digital transformation, says Maldonado to MVS Noticias. Otherwise, there is a risk of generating costly and underutilized infrastructure. He adds that the massive data processing required by the event will take place in local data centers, creating a significant carbon footprint that must be addressed in the tournament's sustainability planning.
Fixed and mobile data consumption during the 2026 World Cup is expected to break all previous records. With global internet penetration now above 70% (compared to 62% in 2022) and a growing number of connected devices, each match will generate hundreds of thousands of simultaneous connections.

title: France, Germany, Ireland And Canada Advise U.S. Tourists to Plan Early for FIFA World Cup 2026 as Record-Breaking Global Travel, Increased Security, and Strained Infrastructure Take Center Stage - Travel And Tour World
snippet: France, Germany, Ireland And Canada Advise U.S. Tourists to Plan Early for FIFA World Cup 2026 as Record-Breaking Global Travel, Increased Security, and Strained Infrastructure Take Center Stage  Travel And Tour World
content:
Published on February 13, 2026
As the FIFA World Cup 2026 approaches, several European countries, including France, Germany, Ireland, and Canada, have issued updated travel advisories for the U.S. due to the anticipated impact of the tournament. These advisories highlight the expected challenges travelers may face, such as heightened security, increased airport congestion, and potential delays. With the tournament being hosted across multiple cities in North America and involving 48 teams, the scale of the event is expected to place significant strain on transportation systems, accommodations, and security protocols. Travelers are urged to plan well in advance, ensure all travel documents are in order, and stay informed of the latest updates to avoid disruptions during this high-demand period.
Several European governments have updated their travel advisories for the United States as the 2026 FIFA World Cup draws nearer, drawing increased attention from tour operators, airlines, and travelers planning international trips around the highly anticipated tournament. The World Cup, set to be hosted across multiple cities in North America with 48 teams participating, is expected to significantly impact both domestic and international travel dynamics.
Countries including France, Germany, Ireland, and Canada have all revised their travel guidelines for the U.S., highlighting potential challenges related to congestion, heightened security, and logistical concerns due to the scale of the event. These advisories don’t discourage travel but urge travelers to stay vigilant, plan their trips well in advance, and adhere strictly to all entry and security protocols.
Travel advisors and airlines are already anticipating the effects of these updates on travel behavior. Although the advisories don’t outright discourage travel, they serve as a reminder to prepare for the busy, high-demand period. Travelers to the U.S. are warned that the World Cup’s extensive fan base will result in crowded venues, airports, and borders, as well as longer wait times for security screenings. Moreover, many host cities are likely to experience a surge in accommodation prices during key match dates, making it essential for international visitors to book well ahead of time.
Advertisement
The revised advisories stress that travelers should ensure their travel documents are in order well before departure. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs, for example, has emphasized the importance of being aware of potential security risks in crowded areas, while also urging travelers to confirm the validity of visas and other necessary authorizations. Germany’s updated guidance echoes these concerns, advising travelers to stay informed through local news channels and official sources during major events such as the World Cup.
These travel warnings are not just limited to international visitors heading to the U.S. U.S.-based travelers are already feeling the ripple effects of the advisories. Some Americans, particularly those planning multi-country European vacations in 2026, are reconsidering their travel plans to avoid the possibility of congestion and soaring prices. Many are either advancing their trips to 2025 or postponing until after the tournament to steer clear of the logistical challenges likely to arise during the World Cup’s peak periods.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Airline analysts have pointed out that even though these advisories do not directly alter policies, the tone of government language can influence how travelers perceive their trips. For risk-averse individuals and families, the updates often result in a shift in booking habits. The mere mention of heightened security and travel disruptions can lead to delays in bookings or a preference for less busy travel periods, especially when it comes to transatlantic flights.
The increased pressure on airlines during the World Cup is expected to have a significant impact on flight availability and prices. With millions of visitors expected to travel between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, European carriers are likely to adjust their summer schedules to prioritize high-demand routes. This could lead to fewer available seats on popular U.S.–Europe flights and result in price hikes, particularly during match windows when demand is at its peak.
Advertisement
Advertisement
At the same time, experts within the travel industry stress that such advisories are a standard part of the process when preparing for large-scale international events. Governments routinely issue these warnings ahead of time to ensure that travelers are well-informed about potential risks, rather than to create panic. The message from European authorities remains consistent: travelers should prepare early, stay updated with official guidance, and be prepared for a spike in demand as the tournament approaches.
For tourism boards and private operators across Europe and North America, these travel advisories are a sign that the 2026 World Cup is already shaping the global tourism landscape. Both sides of the Atlantic are closely monitoring booking data and travel patterns, adjusting strategies accordingly. This intersection of heightened security measures, infrastructure strain, and record-breaking demand is already influencing travel behaviors for 2026 — months ahead of the event itself.
As the World Cup approaches, travelers are advised to stay ahead of the curve. While the advisories serve as a reminder of the logistical and security challenges, they also underscore the importance of early planning. The global attention on the World Cup is undoubtedly transforming how people view travel to the U.S. and other participating countries. The ripple effect of these updates will continue to shape travel decisions well into 2026, setting the stage for a busy, bustling tournament year.

title: Seattle Black Limo Releases Transportation Advisory Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 Match Days at Lumen Field - USA Today
snippet: Seattle Black Limo Releases Transportation Advisory Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 Match Days at Lumen Field  USA Today
content:
February 28, 2026 — Seattle Black Limo, a private chauffeur transportation company operating in Seattle since 2010, has released a transportation advisory related to FIFA World Cup 2026 travel planning. According to the company, visitors, football fans, and corporate travelers may benefit from arranging transportation in advance as match-day traffic demand increases. Travelers can review transportation options through the company’s booking platform at https://www.seattleblacklimo.com/.
Seattle Selected as FIFA World Cup 2026 Host City
Seattle is one of 16 host cities selected across North America for the FIFA World Cup 2026. Six matches are scheduled at Lumen Field between June 15 and July 6. The tournament is expected to bring a high volume of international visitors through Seattle-Tacoma International Airport during the event period. According to publicly available information, increased traffic congestion and parking limitations are expected near Lumen Field on match days.
Transportation Demand Expected to Increase
According to Seattle Black Limo, corporate groups and travel delegations have begun arranging transportation for the tournament period. The company stated that higher demand for rideshare services and increased traffic restrictions around Lumen Field may affect transportation availability and travel times during match days. Travelers planning to attend events may consider arranging transportation ahead of time to support smoother travel planning.
Fleet Preparation for Tournament Period
Seattle Black Limo stated that its chauffeurs monitor flight schedules and traffic conditions to support transportation coordination during busy travel periods. The company’s fleet includes executive sedans, SUVs, and Mercedes Executive Sprinter vans intended to accommodate both individual travelers and groups.
Regional Transportation Coverage
According to the company, transportation services are available across the greater Seattle area, including Bellevue, Redmond, Kirkland, Tacoma, and Everett. Airport meet-and-greet services are also available for travelers arriving at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport.
About Seattle Black Limo
Seattle Black Limo is a licensed and BBB-accredited chauffeur transportation company headquartered at 2640 S Holly St, Seattle, WA 98108. The company operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, providing transportation services including airport transfers, wedding transportation, cruise port transfers at Pier 66 and Pier 91, and long-distance rides to Vancouver, BC and Portland, OR.

title: State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026 - Atlanta News First
snippet: State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026  Atlanta News First
content:
State MARTA committee discusses transportation plans for FIFA World Cup 2026
ATLANTA, Ga. (Atlanta News First) — A state committee in charge of overseeing MARTA held a meeting on Thursday morning to discuss plans for the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Atlanta’s Mercedes-Benz Stadium will host eight games, including a semifinal. City officials estimate the city will see more than 300,000 visitors during the event, which runs from June 11 to July 19.
PREVIOUS COVERAGE: Here are which teams will play in Atlanta during the 2026 World Cup
During the meeting, Jonathan Hunt, MARTA’s interim General Manager and CEO, shared how the transportation agency is preparing to handle large crowds.
“We are very, very excited for the World Cup,” he said.
Hunt said they have a comprehensive plan that includes operational safety, communication enhancements and cybersecurity.
MARTA will offer extended services, as well as increase the number of officers, staff, safety teams and transit ambassadors.
“We’re focused on cleanliness, lighting, additional staffing, and overall appearance of the system,” he said.
Ahead of the World Cup, MARTA will roll out several new services and transit system improvements.
In April, it’ll launch Better Breeze — an easier, faster way to pay.
“You can tap with a credit card. You can get a Breeze card. You can use your smart device if you want. You can use your watch to pay,” Hunt said.
And in the first week of June, MARTA will introduce replacement trains to its existing fleet.
Hunt said the work they’re doing now will not only prepare MARTA for the games, but also benefit the region for years to come.
“Certainly the World Cup was a catalyst, but the vast majority of the money that we’re spending now is going to benefit the region well beyond the World Cup,” Hunt said.
Hunt said they’ll also ensure people can find their way around using multiple languages.

title: Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026 - KCTV
snippet: Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026  KCTV
content:
Kansas City takes steps to ensure transportation for FIFA World Cup 2026
KANSAS CITY, Mo. (KCTV) - There is a new person in charge of making sure Kansas City has adequate transportation when FIFA World Cup 2026 visits the area.
KC2026, the nonprofit organization in charge of planning, and executing, the massive event named Julie Lorenz as Senior Advisor for Transportation.
The organization said Lorenz will work with the transportation team to identify and implement transportation options that will benefit the Kansas City metro for decades to come.
Transportation is a key issue for FIFA World Cup 26. Teams will be spread throughout the region using different sites as practice locations and base camps. As part of the pitch to become a host city, Kansas City leaders ensured FIFA that there would be adequate transportation to handle teams, as well as the thousands of fans who are expected to visit the area.
ALSO READ: Missing gator from petting zoo was found safe after nearly two weeks
“As a longtime member of the Kansas City region, I’m proud to join the team in charge of executing the largest sporting event in this region’s history,” Lorenz said. “The transportation needs of this project are considerable, and I look forward to leveraging my experience in both the public and private sectors to deliver robust transportation plans.”
Kansas City will host six games, including four group-stage matches, one round of 32 and one quarterfinal game. The matches will be played at GEHA Field at Arrowhead Stadium. Construction for the event is already underway at Arrowhead.
The group-stage games are scheduled for:
- Tuesday, June 16th
- Saturday, June 20th
- Thursday, June 25th
- Saturday, June 27th
The round of 32 is scheduled for Friday, July 3. Kansas City will also host a quarter-final matchup on Saturday, July 11.
ALSO READ: Property tax paying troubles, this time for Grandview residents
Other FIFA World Cup 2026 host cities include Atlanta, Boston, Dallas, Guadalajara, Houston, Kansas City, Los Angeles, Mexico City, Miami, Monterrey, New York/New Jersey, Philadelphia, San Francisco, Seattle, Toronto and Vancouver.
The 2026 World Cup will be the first hosted in the US since 1994.

title: TSA to Upgrade Security Screening Technology at FIFA World Cup 2026 Host Airports - Airport Industry-News
snippet: TSA to Upgrade Security Screening Technology at FIFA World Cup 2026 Host Airports  Airport Industry-News
content:
The Transportation Security Administration (TSA) has awarded a multi-million-dollar contract to Rohde & Schwarz for the supply of advanced passenger security screening equipment at US airports in host cities for the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Under the agreement, TSA will deploy Rohde & Schwarz QPS201 advanced imaging technology (AIT) security scanners at selected airport checkpoints. The scanners are intended to support passenger screening operations during the tournament, which will be hosted across multiple cities in the US, Canada and Mexico.
Rohde & Schwarz’s contract covers the delivery of QPS201 systems to airports serving host cities in the US. The award will also contribute to ongoing manufacturing and employment activity at its facilities in Maryland and Texas.
The QPS201 scanner received TSA qualification in 2022, authorising its use at US passenger security screening checkpoints. It is also certified to standards set by TSA and the European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC). More than 100 QPS201 systems are currently in operation at US airports, with over 2,000 deployed globally.
Frank Dunn, CEO of Rohde & Schwarz USA, Inc. said:
We are thrilled to receive this award to deliver QPS201’s high-volume and passenger-friendly on-person security screening technology to modernise checkpoints at the airports of cities hosting the matches. TSA’s continued investment in the QPS will also further expand Rohde & Schwarz’s economic impact as we grow and create jobs at our facilities in Maryland and Texas.
The QPS201 uses millimetre wave radio frequency technology to detect concealed items on passengers. Scans are completed in milliseconds, and the system features an open design with a hands-down scanning position, intended to support accessibility and passenger throughput.
TSA’s investment forms part of broader preparations for major international events expected to increase passenger volumes at US airports over the coming years, including the FIFA World Cup 2026.
Andreas Haegele, Vice President of Microwave Imaging said:
We are proud TSA is investing in modernising security checkpoints at the Soccer World Cup 2026 host city airports with our high-performance QPS201 technology platform. Rohde & Schwarz is deeply committed to our partnership with TSA. We will continue to develop and deliver innovative and effective on-person screening solutions to make airport security more efficient and convenient in the upcoming mega decade of travel including the Soccer World Cup, America's 250th Anniversary and the Olympic Games.

title: CLEAR to Launch eGate Pilot Program at Select U.S. Airports Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026 - Yahoo Finance
snippet: CLEAR to Launch eGate Pilot Program at Select U.S. Airports Ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026  Yahoo Finance
content:
Public-private partnership aims to drive faster, more secure travel at host city airports across the country at no cost to taxpayers
NEW YORK, Aug. 19, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- CLEAR (NYSE: YOU), the secure identity company, today announced the launch of biometric eGates to help modernize America’s airports ahead of the FIFA World Cup 2026 games.
CLEAR is investing to improve security and the traveler experience at no cost to taxpayers. Automated eGates are designed to provide a scalable, secure solution to expedite the passenger experience and enhance security. The innovation builds on the Transportation Security Administration's (TSA) efforts to modernize and streamline U.S. airport security checkpoints, with a specific focus on enhancing security, hospitality, and the passenger experience. This first of its kind pilot represents an innovative public–private partnership that enables TSA to scale identity verification and streamline airport processing just in time for the World Cup. With more than 20 million international visitors expected to fly to the U.S. for the tournament — and domestic travel surging through U.S. host cities — checkpoint infrastructure will face unprecedented pressure. As part of this strategic partnership, CLEAR is investing to improve security and the traveler experience at no cost to taxpayers.
Starting in August 2025, these eGates will debut at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (ATL), Ronald Reagan National Airport (DCA), and Seattle–Tacoma International Airport (SEA), with CLEAR planning to expand the eGate implementation across its nationwide network of airports in preparation for the World Cup, America’s 250th anniversary celebration, and continuing growth in domestic air travel.
“We are proud to help make America’s airports great ahead of the World Cup. The U.S. should be leading on security, hospitality, and the travel experience,” said CLEAR CEO Caryn Seidman Becker. “This partnership with TSA is a powerful example of how public and private sectors can work together to enhance security and improve the aviation ecosystem. CLEAR is investing in this technology at no cost to taxpayers to modernize our checkpoints and ensure America’s airports are ready to meet global expectations. CLEAR’s secure identity platform will help unlock a better, safer, and more frictionless experience for all travelers.”
CLEAR’s eGates are designed to conduct real-time biometric verification, matching a traveler’s facial image with their identity document and boarding pass. TSA retains complete operational control: triggering gate access, conducting security vetting, and enforcing government security requirements. CLEAR transmits only limited data (live photo, boarding pass, ID photo used for enrollment and identity information)--it has no access to watchlists, cannot override TSA gate decisions, and does not manually open gates. Once identity and clearance are confirmed, passengers proceed directly to physical screening, bypassing the TSA podium while still undergoing all security screening.
About CLEAR
CLEAR's mission is to strengthen security and create frictionless experiences. With over 33 million Members and a growing network of partners across the world, CLEAR's secure identity platform is transforming the way people live, work, and travel. Whether you are traveling, at the stadium, or on your phone, CLEAR connects you to the things that make you, you—making everyday experiences easier, more secure, and friction-free. CLEAR is committed to privacy done right. Members are always in control of their own information, and we never sell Member data. For more information, visit clearme.com.
Forward-Looking Statements

This includes, without limitation, statements related to the planned pilot of eGates and related expansion plans. title: Airbnb is offering $750 USD to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts - Airbnb Newsroom
snippet: Airbnb is offering $750 USD to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts  Airbnb Newsroom
content:
Airbnb is offering $750 USD to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb launches its biggest new host incentive program ever
- Airbnb is seeking new hosts to meet surging demand for stays during the FIFA World Cup™
- Hosts could earn $3,000 USD on average by renting their space during the tournament, according to Deloitte
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb launches its biggest new host incentive program ever
- Airbnb is seeking new hosts to meet surging demand for stays during the FIFA World Cup™
- Hosts could earn $3,000 USD on average by renting their space during the tournament, according to Deloitte
With the FIFA World Cup 2026™ on the horizon, millions of people around the world are eagerly making travel plans to attend the biggest sporting event on the planet.
Airbnb – Official FIFA World Cup 2026™ Tournament Supporter – is looking for new hosts and has today launched its biggest new host incentive program ever. Under the offer, new entire home hosts in any of the 16 World Cup host cities in Canada, Mexico and the US who welcome their first guests between now and July 31, 2026 are eligible to receive $750 USD.
The launch comes as new data shows that demand for stays on Airbnb during the FIFA World Cup™ is surging, with searches for stays in host cities increasing by an average of 80% compared to the same period last year.1
More information on the new host incentive, including details on eligibility, is available at airbnb.com/new-host-fifa.
An opportunity for residents of host cities
Millions of fans are expected to attend the FIFA World Cup™ this summer and Deloitte estimates that residents of host cities could earn $3,000 USD on average by renting their space on Airbnb during the tournament.2
And survey insights from Focaldata3 released today show what the extra income from hosting would mean to residents of host cities. It shows that potential new hosts are motivated by opportunities to boost their income to help afford basic living costs, and to share their communities with guests. The Focaldata survey shows:
- Almost two-thirds of respondents (64%) would consider renting out space in their homes during the tournament;
- Almost half (49%) say earning extra income would be their primary motivation for hosting;
- Almost 90% say the extra income would have a significant or moderate positive impact on their current financial situation;
- Residents would mostly use the extra income to help clear debts (40%), cover living expenses (35%), and make home improvements (33%);
- Residents would be most excited to share local hidden gems (52%) and their favourite restaurants, cafes and attractions (49%) with guests.
Dave Stephenson, Chief Business Officer at Airbnb, said:
“As the world’s biggest sporting event comes to North America, Airbnb is launching its biggest new host incentive ever. Demand for World Cup stays on Airbnb is surging, giving residents of host cities the opportunity to boost their incomes by sharing their homes and the communities they love. There’s truly never been a better time to become a host on Airbnb.”
Support for new hosts
Airbnb has a number of resources to support new hosts on the platform:
- Explore the Resource Center. Learn more about hosting during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, including tips for new hosts and ways to attract guests.
- Find a Co-Host. Get high-quality local support through the Co-Host Network. You can ask a co-host to handle specific tasks. Their services can include helping with licensing and hosting permits, listing setup, setting prices and availability, and cleaning and maintenance.
- Join a local Host Club. Connect with other hosts, share insights, and stay informed about exclusive events for hosts related to the tournament.

title: Airbnb is offering $1,000 to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts in Canada - Airbnb Newsroom
snippet: Airbnb is offering $1,000 to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts in Canada  Airbnb Newsroom
content:
Airbnb is offering $1,000 to new FIFA World Cup 2026™ hosts in Canada
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb launches its biggest new host incentive program ever
- Airbnb is seeking new hosts to meet surging demand for stays during the FIFA World Cup 2026™
- Hosts could earn an average of $2,700 in Toronto and $4,200 in Vancouver by renting their space during the tournament, according to Deloitte
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb launches its biggest new host incentive program ever
- Airbnb is seeking new hosts to meet surging demand for stays during the FIFA World Cup 2026™
- Hosts could earn an average of $2,700 in Toronto and $4,200 in Vancouver by renting their space during the tournament, according to Deloitte
With the FIFA World Cup 2026™ on the horizon, millions of people around the world are eagerly making travel plans to come to Canada and attend the biggest sporting event on the planet.
Airbnb—Official FIFA World Cup 2026™ Tournament Supporter—is looking for new hosts and has today launched its biggest new host incentive program ever. Under the offer, new entire home hosts in any of the 16 World Cup host cities in Canada, the US, and Mexico who welcome their first guests between now and July 31, 2026 are eligible to receive approximately $1,000 CAD ($750 USD).
The launch comes as new data shows that demand for stays on Airbnb during the FIFA World Cup is surging, with searches for stays in host cities increasing by an average of 80 per cent compared to the same period last year.1
More information on the new host incentive, including details on eligibility, is available at http://airbnb.com/new-host-fifa.
An opportunity for Toronto and Vancouver residents
Millions of fans are expected to attend the FIFA World Cup this summer, including 146,000 fans in Toronto and 204,000 fans in Vancouver.2 Deloitte estimates that Toronto residents could earn about $2,700 on average by renting their space on Airbnb during the tournament, while hosts in Vancouver could earn roughly $4,200.3
And survey insights from Focaldata4 released today show what the extra income from hosting would mean for Canadians. It shows that potential new hosts are motivated by opportunities to boost their income to help afford basic living costs, and to share their communities with guests. The Focaldata survey shows:
- Almost two-thirds of respondents (63 per cent) would consider renting out space in their homes during the tournament;
- Over half (54 per cent) say earning extra income would be their primary motivation for hosting;
- Almost 90 per cent say the extra income would have a significant or moderate positive impact on their current financial situation;
- Residents would mostly use the extra income to help clear debts (42 per cent), cover living expenses (33 per cent), and help save mores (29 per cent);
- Residents would be most excited to share local hidden gems (53 per cent) and their favourite restaurants, cafes and attractions (52 per cent) with guests.
Dave Stephenson, Chief Business Officer at Airbnb, said:
“As the world’s biggest sporting event comes to North America, Airbnb is launching its biggest new host incentive ever. Demand for World Cup stays on Airbnb is surging, giving residents of host cities the opportunity to boost their incomes by sharing their homes and the communities they love. There’s truly never been a better time to become a host on Airbnb.”
Support for new hosts
Airbnb has a number of resources to support new hosts on the platform:
- Explore the Resource Centre. Learn more about hosting during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, including tips for new hosts and ways to attract guests.
- Find a Co-Host. Get high-quality local support through the Co-Host Network. You can ask a co-host to handle specific tasks. Their services can include helping with licensing and hosting permits, listing setup, setting prices and availability, and cleaning and maintenance.
- Join a local Host Club.Connect with other hosts, share insights, and stay informed about exclusive events for hosts related to the tournament.

title: Supporting nine FIFA World Cup 2026™ fan zones across Washington - Airbnb Newsroom
snippet: Supporting nine FIFA World Cup 2026™ fan zones across Washington  Airbnb Newsroom
content:
Supporting nine FIFA World Cup 2026™ fan zones across Washington
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb is supporting Official SeattleFWC26 Fan Zones across Washington state through grants to nine communities as part of its Host City Impact Program tied to the FIFA World Cup 2026™.
- The “World to Washington” initiative is designed to bring World Cup excitement, tourism, and economic opportunity to communities beyond Seattle.
Key Takeaways
- Airbnb is supporting Official SeattleFWC26 Fan Zones across Washington state through grants to nine communities as part of its Host City Impact Program tied to the FIFA World Cup 2026™.
- The “World to Washington” initiative is designed to bring World Cup excitement, tourism, and economic opportunity to communities beyond Seattle.
As Washington prepares to welcome the world for the FIFA World Cup 2026, Airbnb is announcing a new community investment initiative designed to bring the excitement – and economic benefits – of the tournament to communities across the state.
Through the World to Washington initiative, Airbnb is providing $40,000 grants to Official Seattle FIFA World Cup 2026™ Fan Zones in Bellingham, Bremerton, Everett, Olympia, Spokane, Tacoma, Tri-Cities, Vancouver, and Yakima.
In total, Airbnb’s grants to Official SeattleFWC26 Fan Zones across Washington state represent a more than $360,000 contribution supporting local celebrations, community gathering spaces, and fan-focused programming during the tournament.
“The FIFA World Cup is a huge moment for Washington—something we might only see once in a generation. At Airbnb, we want to help everyone in our state be part of the excitement. That’s why we’re proud to be supporting Fan Zones, so people all across Washington can experience the tournament close to home. It’s a great way for local businesses and communities to share in the energy and opportunities this incredible event brings.”
Airbnb Chief Business Officer Dave Stephenson
“Airbnb’s generous contribution is exactly the kind of community investment that will make Seattle’s World Cup experience truly special. This support helps us bring the energy and excitement of the tournament directly to neighborhoods across Washington, creating fan experiences where locals and visitors alike can come together to celebrate the beautiful game.”
Peter Tomozawa, CEO, SeattleFWC26
“We’re grateful to Airbnb for sponsoring Bellingham as a destination for soccer fans this summer. We can’t wait for people from across the country and the world to experience the watch parties and business activations our community has in store—as well as the incredible trails, parks, and people that make our corner of Washington State so special.”
Dylan Deane-Boyle, President and CEO, Visit Bellingham
“With Airbnb as our presenting sponsor, we’re transforming downtown Bremerton into a world-class Fan Zone — complete with a giant LED screen at Quincy Square, family-friendly activities, beer gardens, food trucks, and unforgettable match-day energy that makes it easy to turn a ferry ride from Seattle into an overnight Bremerton experience.”
Jessica Combs, Executive Director of the Downtown Bremerton Association and Activation Manager for the Official Bremerton SeattleFWC26 Fan Zone
“The local fan zone host committee would like to thank Airbnb for their generous grant supporting Fan Zones across the State. These monies will help us enhance our fan zone experiences to engage our community and travelers who wish to join us in Yakima for the Fan Zone experience.”
Yakima City Councilmember Patricia Byers
“Spokane is proud to host the SeattleFWC26 Fan Zone, creating opportunities for our community to come together and experience the excitement of the World Cup right here at home. Partnership with Airbnb help make these moments approachable, welcoming, and community-driven.”
Ashley Blake, CEO, Spokane Sports
“We’re proud to partner with Airbnb as a sponsor of our Everett Fan Zone! This collaboration highlights Everett as a welcoming destination for soccer fans from near and far while also creating meaningful economic opportunities for our local businesses. Together, we are preparing to bring the world to Everett and showcase our city’s beauty, spirit and hospitality at our waterfront Fan Zone this June.”
City of Everett Mayor Cassie Franklin
Supporting communities through the Host City Impact Program
World to Washington is part of Airbnb’s $5 million Host City Impact Program, which builds on Airbnb’s multi-tournament partnership with FIFA to support fans and communities leading up to the FIFA World Cup 2026™.
Through the Host City Impact Program, Airbnb is collaborating with select cities to identify projects that foster economic growth, strengthen neighborhoods, and enhance the overall experience for residents and visitors during major sporting events and beyond.
FIFA World Cup 2026™ travel expected to deliver $260M in economic impact in Seattle
Recent analysis underscores the scale of the FIFA World Cup 2026™ tournament’s anticipated impact across the region.
During the tournament period, Airbnb hosts in and around Seattle are projected to welcome approximately 23,000 guests, helping absorb demand beyond traditional hotel zones and spreading visitor spending into local communities.
According to analysis conducted by Deloitte,1 Airbnb guest travel tied to the World Cup in Seattle is expected to generate:
- An estimated $260M in total economic output across Seattle and nearby areas
- Nearly $19M in total host earnings, averaging roughly $3,800 per host during the tournament window
- Roughly $138M in value added to GDP in and around Seattle.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026™ travel trends, revealed - Airbnb Newsroom
snippet: FIFA World Cup 2026™ travel trends, revealed  Airbnb Newsroom
content:
FIFA World Cup 2026™ travel trends, revealed
Key Takeaways
- Guests from the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada are driving the most searches for stays during the match dates.
- Families and groups account for more than half of World Cup trips, to date.
- Across host cities, a significant percentage of available listings are priced under $500 per night, and early guest booking trends reflect the preference for affordable listings under this price point as well.
Key Takeaways
- Guests from the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada are driving the most searches for stays during the match dates.
- Families and groups account for more than half of World Cup trips, to date.
- Across host cities, a significant percentage of available listings are priced under $500 per night, and early guest booking trends reflect the preference for affordable listings under this price point as well.
As excitement for FIFA World Cup 2026™ builds, travel planning is already in full swing—with demand surging for early matches featuring legendary matchups and underdog face-offs. Airbnb is sharing insights into World Cup travel patterns from guest demographics, to the emergence of ‘gateway’ travel, where fans seize the opportunity to explore beyond match venues and discover hidden gems across the host countries.
Matches driving the most demand
Overall, guests from the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada are driving the most searches for stays surrounding the match dates1. Below are some of the matches that have driven the most demand for stays on Airbnb, as well as the countries driving the most searches for trips during the tournament:
Families and groups drive early travel demand
Our data reveals that the World Cup is drawing families and groups from around the world to participate in this once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. In fact, families and groups account for more than half of World Cup trips, to date4.
- Among families booking for the World Cup, Los Angeles is the most popular host city they are traveling to, with San Francisco, Seattle, Vancouver, Miami, and Kansas City also seeing high demand5.
- Two-night stays are most common among this group, representing nearly 30% of bookings, followed by four-to-five-night stays (over 20%)6.
- The largest number of family bookings are coming from travelers based in Chicago, Arlington, VA, and Los Angeles, showing strong interest from major US cities7.
- Many families are choosing larger, more affordable homes, with approximately 75% of family bookings being for two-and three-bedroom listings8.
Gen Z fans are traveling the distance, together
The tournament is proving especially popular with younger travelers. A closer look at their travel patterns reveals a strong interest from international Gen Z guests, and a preference to experience the World Cup together in groups:
- Gen Z guests account for more than 15% of bookings, with nearly half traveling in groups, reflecting the social nature of World Cup travel9.
- Los Angeles, Miami, and New York rank highest among Gen Z travelers, of which over 40% have booked trips lasting three-to-five nights10.
- International interest is also notable, with London and Glasgow emerging as leading origin cities for Gen Z guests, followed by Tokyo and Los Angeles11.
Guests embrace gateway travel to explore destinations beyond the tournament
Many travelers are using the World Cup tournament as a launching pad for further travel in host countries, especially those coming from farther afield. In fact, non-US travelers visiting during the World Cup account for 70 percent of gateway travel resulting from the tournament12, and are driving other interesting trends:
- Non-US guests are visiting more destinations during their World Cup trips than US guests, and are also staying an average of three nights longer13.
- Latin and South American travelers are staying longer, 16 nights, on average, followed by travelers from Europe (14 nights), Asia Pacific (13 nights) and the US and Canada (10 nights)14.
- Travelers from Argentina are taking the longest trips of any country, while travelers from Germany are visiting the most destinations of any country, during their trips15.
Affordable stays still available near the action
As an Official FIFA World Cup 2026™ Supporter, Airbnb is helping host city residents earn additional income by listing their space during the World Cup. The beauty of Airbnb is the diversity of affordable options and value that our hosts provide.
Across host cities, a significant percentage of available listings are priced under $500 per night, and early guest booking trends reflect the preference for affordable listings under this price point as well16. We’re also seeing that a large share of available two-or-more bedroom listings are less than $500/night17. These stays offer more space and better value for fans and families who want to stay together.
To inspire trip planning, we have identified some Guest Favorite stays within easy reach of the official stadiums and under $500 per night18—from family-friendly spacious stays, to design-forward city lofts, to relaxing private retreats—offering a first look at where travelers want to stay during one of the world’s biggest sporting events.
These unique homes reflect travelers’ rising demand for local experiences while seeing their favorite teams play on the world stage. According to a recent Deloitte study19, Airbnb guests are projected to generate $3.6 billion for host city economies during the tournament, underscoring the positive impact of home sharing for communities. Guests that are planning their travels for the tournament can discover unique places to stay and things to do at airbnb.com/fifa.

For group travel, 67 per cent of available two bedroom or larger listings are under $500 per night with a significant percentage of bookings to date also under that price point.

For group travel, nearly 50 per cent of available two bedroom or larger listings are under $500 per night with a significant percentage of bookings to date also under that price point.

Overall, guests from Canada, the United States, and United Kingdom are driving the most searches for stays surrounding the match dates7. Among Canadians, demand has been strongest around domestic matches—particularly in Vancouver. Canadians are also searching during World Cup matches in other countries. Some of the host cities seeing the biggest World Cup travel surge in searches by Canadians include Monterrey and Guadalajara in Mexico as well as Kansas City in the U.S.10

Our data reveals that the World Cup is drawing families and groups from around the world to participate in this once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. In fact, families and groups account for more than half of World Cup trips by Canadians, to date11.
- Among Canadian families booking for the World Cup, San Francisco is the most popular host city they are travelling to, with Seattle, Miami and Vancouver also seeing high demand12.
- Two-night stays are most common among this group, representing over 30 per cent of bookings, followed by four- to five-night stays (nearly 20 per cent)13.
Gen Z fans are travelling the distance, together
The tournament is proving especially popular with younger travellers. A closer look at their travel patterns reveals a strong interest from international Gen Z guests, and a preference to experience the World Cup together in groups:
- Gen Z guests account for more than 15 per cent of bookings made by Canadians, with nearly half travelling in groups, reflecting the social nature of World Cup travel14.
- Los Angeles, Houston and Miami rank highest among destinations for Canadian Gen Z travellers, of which the majority are booking two-or-three night trips15.
- International interest across all host cities is also notable, with London and Glasgow emerging as leading origin cities for Gen Z guests, followed by Tokyo and Los Angeles16.

title: Airbnbs to contribute estimated $70 million to Atlanta’s economy during FIFA World Cup 2026 - Atlanta News First
snippet: Airbnbs to contribute estimated $70 million to Atlanta’s economy during FIFA World Cup 2026  Atlanta News First
content:
Airbnbs to contribute estimated $70 million to Atlanta’s economy during FIFA World Cup 2026
ATLANTA, Ga. (Atlanta News First) - Hundreds of thousands of people are expected to flood Atlanta for the FIFA World Cup 2026 — but not all of them will stay in traditional hotels.
Airbnb said local hosts are expected to welcome about 6,000 guests, who will contribute an estimated $70 million to Atlanta’s economy.
The short-term home rental company is currently FIFA’s Official Alternative Accommodations and Experiences Booking Platform, starting with the FIFA World Cup 2026 and ending with the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2027.
Across all 16 host cities for the 2026 world cup, Airbnb expects stays will contribute $3.6 billion to local economies.
Atlanta is scheduled to host eight matches, including a semi-final, at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. FIFA will announce the placement of the 48 teams this weekend.
The third round of FIFA World Cup 2026 lottery ticket sales begins on Dec. 11 and ends on Jan. 13.

title: Newark’s Best Airbnbs for Easy Access to the FIFA World Cup 2026 - Condé Nast Traveler
snippet: Newark’s Best Airbnbs for Easy Access to the FIFA World Cup 2026  Condé Nast Traveler
content:
Newark’s Best Airbnbs for Easy Access to the FIFA World Cup 2026
Newark, New Jersey, is a city with many sides. As one of the country’s oldest urban centers, it blends historic architecture with a lively arts scene. Portuguese bakeries sit close to new developments, while Newark Penn Station pulses with activity. Exhibits at the Newark Museum of Contemporary Art, live shows at the New Jersey Performing Arts Center, and Devils hockey games at the Prudential Center add to its appeal. Newark isn’t just a stop on the way to New York City—or a place to stay while attending the 2026 FIFA World Cup—it’s a gateway, a meeting place, and a destination with its own energy.
No matter your reason for visiting, you can find an Airbnb in Newark that fits your needs. Choose a modern apartment in the Ironbound neighborhood near popular rodízio restaurants, or pick a large home perfect for groups before a flight from Newark Liberty International Airport. There are stylish lofts close to University Heights and quiet homes minutes from public transit that will bring you to Manhattan, MetLife Stadium, and beyond. With so many options, it’s easy to explore all that the tristate area has to offer. Whatever your travel style, a Newark Airbnb is waiting for you.
We've selected these listings based on Superhost status, ratings, amenities, location, decor, editor stays, and previous guest reviews.
Our top picks:
- Best for design lovers: Converted button factory loft in the heart of the Ironbound
- Best for NYC commuters: Modern three-bedroom retreat near downtown Newark
- Best for multi-family stays: Expansive seven-bedroom home near Branch Brook Park
FAQ:
What are some exciting events happening in Newark?
Newark’s events calendar is always stacked, from year‑round concerts at the Prudential Center (Cardi B is coming in March!) to performances at NJPAC and festivals throughout the city. One of the biggest highlights for 2026 is the FIFA World Cup tournament at MetLife Stadium in Rutherford, New Jersey (approximately 20 minutes by car from Newark), which will be hosting multiple soccer matches, making Newark an ideal home base thanks to its quick transit connections, ample accommodations, and wow-worthy dining scene. Whether you’re in town for global sports, a Devils hockey game, or a major concert, Newark puts you close to the action.
Is Newark a good base for visiting NYC?
Yes, Newark is one of the most convenient and affordable gateways from NYC. Newark Penn Station offers direct trains to Manhattan, and many Newark neighborhoods are just a 20–30 minute ride from Midtown Manhattan. It’s a smart choice for travelers who want easy access to the city without the Manhattan price tag.
Is Newark family‑friendly?
Absolutely. Branch Brook Park, the Newark Museum of Art, and nearby attractions like the American Dream Mall make it easy to keep kids entertained. Many Airbnbs in the area offer multiple bedrooms, bunk‑bed setups, game rooms, and outdoor spaces. As with any city, neighborhoods vary—stick to well-reviewed areas like the Ironbound District, Forest Hill, or near Branch Brook Park, where you’ll find walkable streets, kid-friendly amenities, and a welcoming community vibe.

title: Robin Radar to support counter-UAS operations at FIFA World Cup 2026 - sUAS News
snippet: Robin Radar to support counter-UAS operations at FIFA World Cup 2026  sUAS News
content:
Robin Radar to support counter-UAS operations at FIFA World Cup 2026
Virginia, United States: Robin Radar Systems today announces that its conflict-proven IRIS drone-detection radar will be deployed by components of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS), alongside state and local agencies, as part of counter-UAS operations for the FIFA World Cup in North America later this year. The radars will be responsible for protecting high-visibility events and critical infrastructure following additional support from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA).
The deployment reflects growing recognition across U.S. homeland security agencies that drone threats at large-scale public events require persistent, reliable airspace awareness, delivered through scalable, easy-to-operate sensing systems.
The IRIS is a plug-and-play radar solution and can be set up in under 15 minutes by a single operator without technical support and operates 24/7 seamlessly with minimal human input. Unlike more complex military systems that require teams of engineers to deploy and operate, the IRIS radar integrates seamlessly into broader counter-UAS architectures alongside cameras, acoustic sensors, passive detection systems and command-and-control platforms.
The FIFA World Cup deployment builds on Robin Radar’s role in securing major international events, including the Paris Olympic Games in 2024, where IRIS was integrated into a multi-sensor security framework protecting athletes and spectators.
Robin Radar’s IRIS provides 360-degree situational awareness, detecting, tracking, and classifying airborne objects, including drones deliberately designed to evade traditional detection methods. With an instrumented range of up to 12 kilometres, IRIS offers early warning, giving authorities the time and space required to assess threats, coordinate responses, and act decisively.
IRIS is a TRL 9 capability, meaning it has been used extensively in active conflict environments, providing operational validation in high-pressure settings. That experience has informed product refinement and accelerated scaling, applying conflict-proven technology to the domestic setting.
Robin Radar has seen exponential growth across North America in 2025, driven by demand from DHS components and Canadian federal agencies, as major political and sporting events continue to require increased airspace security. The company has also partnered with multiple prime integrators under forthcoming DHS Counter-UAS procurement frameworks, reinforcing its position as a trusted radar supplier within the wider CUAS ecosystem.
Kristian Brost, General Manager, Robin Radar North America, said: “DHS components and state authorities are looking for solutions that work immediately, that scale, and that can be operated by non-technical teams. IRIS delivers exactly that. It’s a plug-and-play radar that provides persistent airspace awareness within minutes of arrival. At an event the size of the World Cup, clarity and early warnings are critical.”
Marcel Verdonk, Chief Commercial Officer at Robin Radar Systems, said: “Large-scale public events require trusted, off-the-shelf security technology that can be delivered at pace. We have demonstrated that we can supply in quantity, from supporting European ministries of defence to delivering at scale in North America. IRIS provides reliable military-grade detection with rapid deployment, and that reassurance matters when agencies are protecting millions of people.”

Senator John Hoeven announced the activation of the Vantis Federal Radar Data Enclave, a secure integration of unfiltered radar data from the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) with the Vantis system at the Northern Plains Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) Test Site.
February 25, 2026
Northrop Grumman and the U.S. Air Force are accelerating B‑21 Raider production following strong performance in ground and flight testing. Supported by more than $5 billion in digital engineering and manufacturing investments, the next‑generation stealth bomber remains on track for first delivery to Ellsworth AFB in 2027. The B‑21 features sixth‑generation stealth, advanced manufacturing processes and an open architecture for rapid upgrades, making it a cornerstone of future U.S. airpower.
By Parallel Flight Technologies
February 25, 2026
Parallel Flight Technologies has secured an FAA 44807 exemption, allowing commercial operation of its Firefly heavy‑lift hybrid unmanned aircraft system in the U.S. The decision enables missions such as wildland fire support, logistics, and heavy‑power sensor work. Firefly’s hybrid propulsion system supports 100‑pound payloads and high endurance, backed by significant government agency support. The company expects to begin customer deliveries this summer and will expand production to meet rising demand.
February 24, 2026
Aptima will continue facilitating NASA’s RAM‑AO Working Group as it develops frameworks, research and standards to scale autonomous multi‑aircraft UAS operations in the national airspace. The group meets March 3–5 at NASA Ames to advance work on safety modeling, policy gaps, crew design, validation frameworks and integrated airspace management.

title: CBP Shares Travel Guidance for FIFA World Cup 2026 - Homeland Security Today
snippet: CBP Shares Travel Guidance for FIFA World Cup 2026  Homeland Security Today
content:
U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) has released new travel guidance ahead of the FIFA World Cup 2026™, which kicks off in the U.S. in Los Angeles on 12 June, as millions of visitors are expected to cross U.S. borders for matches and related events.
CBP said it is coordinating with DHS and other partners to support secure and efficient travel for athletes, officials, fans, and international visitors, while maintaining public safety through planning, resource deployment, and threat monitoring across land, air, and sea ports of entry.
In its “Know Before You Go” fact sheet, CBP encouraged eligible travelers to use expedited processing options such as Global Entry and the Mobile Passport Control (MPC) app, along with trusted traveler programs like NEXUS and SENTRI.
CBP also reminded travelers to ensure they have the proper documentation before arrival. Passports should generally be valid for six months beyond the intended stay, unless covered by an agreement with the U.S. Travelers using the Visa Waiver Program must have ESTA approval prior to travel, including for children.
The guidance includes reminders on items and activities that can cause delays or enforcement actions, including requirements to declare all food, restrictions on medications, and reporting rules for travelers carrying more than $10,000. CBP also noted that foreign visitors cannot bring firearms into the U.S. without authorization, and weapons are prohibited at stadiums.
CBP additionally warned that using drones at sporting events or mass gatherings is illegal, and violations may result in enforcement action or criminal prosecution.

title: Houston non-profit builds emergency safe home for sex trafficking victims leading up to FIFA World Cup 2026 - FOX 26 Houston
snippet: Houston non-profit builds emergency safe home for sex trafficking victims leading up to FIFA World Cup 2026  FOX 26 Houston
content:
Houston non-profit builds emergency safe home for sex trafficking victims leading up to FIFA World Cup 2026
HOUSTON - With the influx of people expected to be in town during the summer for FIFA World Cup 2026, members of Elijah Rising and Eight Days of Hope plan on having a safe home ready for trafficking victims by the first week of March. Members of Elijah Rising say they hope the emergency safe home built isn't full during the matches held in the city, but realistically say it will probably be packed.
Emergency safe house for trafficking victim
What they're saying:
Elijah Rising partnered together with Eight Days of Hope to create the Emergency Safe Home.
Eight Days of Hope started construction on February 16th and plan on taking about two weeks to complete the work.
Once the Emergency Safe Home is complete, it will have four rooms available, each with two twin beds. This means that a total of eight victims can be sheltered during an emergency.
The safe home will be furnished and have a kitchen and common area for victims to feel more ‘at home’, according to Elijah Rising.
Other anti-trafficking plans
Dig deeper:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 Houston Host Committee published the first version of their Human Rights Action Plan, where they explain what prevention measures they have in place regarding a possible spike in sex trafficking during the matches being held in Houston.
The plan focuses on three primary pillars: Inclusion and Safeguarding, Workers' Rights, and Access to Remedy.
In partnership with A21, the committee offers 35-minute training sessions for the hospitality, transportation, and volunteer sectors to identify and prevent trafficking.
Specific training is provided to ensure staff and volunteers can support fans with disabilities, and a campaign running from May to July 2026 will raise awareness about gender-based violence and buyer awareness.
Resources
What you can do:
If you or someone you know is a victim of human trafficking, there are several options to seek help.
The Source: Elijah Rising, Eight Days of Hope, FIFA World Cup 2026 Houston Host Committee

content:

This move can probably ruin the excitement linked to the coveted football tournament, as fans from the countries that are on the list and have qualified for the tournament will not be able to cheer for the fans from inside the stadium. There are also chances that the majority of matches will be played in the United States; this could turn out to be a problem for people from countries on the list.
Which countries are on Trump's travel ban list?
At the time of announcing the travel ban, the US president announced that he would introduce 'common sense restrictions' on travel to the US that would 'protect Americans from dangerous foreign actors,' according to the online platform Lad Bible. The travel ban impacts people from the following countries:
- Afghanistan
- Myanmar
- Chad
- Congo-Brazzaville
- Equatorial Guinea
- Eritrea
- Haiti
- Iran
- Libya
- Somalia
- Sudan
- Yemen
It is also pertinent to mention that partial travel restrictions were also imposed on travelers from Burundi, Cuba, Laos, Sierra Leone, Togo, Turkmenistan, and Venezuela.

What will happen to countries that qualified for the 2026 World Cup?
Two out of 19 countries on the travel ban list have qualified for the 2026 World Cup: Iran and Haiti. For Iran, it was the seventh time ever that the Middle Eastern nation has earned itself a spot in the tournament after topping their group with 23 points. Meanwhile, the other team that qualified was Haiti, which is also subject to Trump's travel ban, and they also sealed their second World Cup qualification after a 51-year drought.
Are players of the banned countries allowed in the US?
The players of the two banned nations will be fighting it out on the pitch, as they are set to be granted an entry, though due to an exemption.
What does the exemption say?
The exemptions underscore that 'any athlete or member of an athletic team, including coaches, persons performing a necessary support role, and immediate relatives, traveling for the World Cup, Olympics, or other major sporting event as determined by the Secretary of State' are allowed in—but this doesn't apply to fans.

title: Senegal Alongwith Haiti, Ivory Coast and Iran are at the Crossroads of International Politics as Fans Face U.S. Travel Ban and Restrictions, Political Barriers Threaten to Crush FIFA World Cup 2026 Dreams! - Travel And Tour World
snippet: Senegal Alongwith Haiti, Ivory Coast and Iran are at the Crossroads of International Politics as Fans Face U.S. Travel Ban and Restrictions, Political Barriers Threaten to Crush FIFA World Cup 2026 Dreams!  Travel And Tour World
content:
Published on January 14, 2026
Senegal, along with Haiti, Ivory Coast, and Iran, are at a critical crossroads of international politics as they face major challenges ahead of the FIFA 2026 World Cup. Fans from these nations are caught in a web of U.S. travel bans and restrictions, which could prevent them from attending the tournament in the United States. The political barriers imposed by the U.S. have created uncertainty, threatening to crush the FIFA 2026 dreams of passionate supporters who have been waiting for this moment. These restrictions not only affect ordinary fans but also create a sense of injustice, as political factors overshadow the spirit of football.
As the World Cup draws closer, the stakes are higher than ever for Senegal, Haiti, Ivory Coast, and Iran, who may be forced to watch from afar, unable to support their teams on one of the world’s grandest stages. The situation is a stark reminder of the intersection between sports and politics.
Iran has qualified for the 2026 World Cup, marking their fourth consecutive appearance at the prestigious tournament. The Iranian team has been steadily improving over the years, with players like Mehdi Taremi and Sardar Azmoun becoming household names in world football. Their performance during the qualification rounds was impressive, and they now find themselves in a group with Belgium, Egypt, and New Zealand — all formidable opponents.
However, there’s a significant issue looming over Iran’s journey to the World Cup: their fans are banned from entering the United States, where the bulk of the 2026 tournament will be held. Due to current U.S. government policies, ordinary citizens of Iran will be unable to obtain tourist or fan visas. This means that while the players and coaching staff will be able to attend, the passionate supporters — who are known for their enthusiasm and dedication — will be left behind.
Advertisement
The exclusion of Iranian fans from the U.S. is a major blow, especially considering the rich football culture in Iran. Every World Cup is an opportunity for the Iranian people to showcase their love for the game, but for 2026, this dream may not come true for many. The team will face this monumental challenge with the knowledge that their most loyal supporters won’t be there to cheer them on in the stands.
Haiti’s qualification for the 2026 World Cup marks a historic moment for the country, as it will be only their second appearance at the tournament, after a 52-year gap. This remarkable achievement is even more impressive when you consider the many obstacles Haiti has faced, both on and off the pitch. The Haitian team has worked tirelessly to earn their spot, topping their CONCACAF qualifier group. With key players like Duckens Nazon and Frantzdy Pierrot, Haiti is ready to make a strong impact in the 2026 tournament.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Unfortunately, the celebrations for this achievement are bittersweet. Like Iran, Haitian fans face significant challenges in attending the World Cup in the United States. Due to U.S. travel restrictions, many Haitian supporters may be unable to secure visas to travel to the tournament. This ban is devastating for the country’s fans, who have always been a driving force behind the national team. While some games will take place in Canada, where travel restrictions are less stringent, the inability to attend the U.S. matches will still leave a large portion of the Haitian fanbase unable to witness their country’s most important sporting event in person.
For Haiti, the World Cup is not just about football — it’s about pride, unity, and the opportunity to showcase the nation’s resilience. But with many fans excluded from the stands, the journey to 2026 will feel incomplete.
Advertisement
Advertisement
Côte d’Ivoire (Ivory Coast) is another African powerhouse that has secured its place in the 2026 World Cup. The Ivorian team has a proud history, with famous players like Didier Drogba and Yaya Touré having played for the national team in the past. Now, the current squad, led by stars like Nicolas Pépé and Wilfried Zaha, is aiming to take the nation to new heights in 2026. Côte d’Ivoire has been drawn into a competitive group featuring Germany, Curaçao, and Ecuador — and while the competition will be tough, the Ivorians are well prepared to make their mark.
Despite their qualification, Ivorian fans face a similar fate to Iranian and Haitian supporters. The U.S. travel ban means that fans who want to see their team play on American soil will face major obstacles. The restrictions apply to regular citizens from Ivory Coast, making it difficult for them to secure visas to attend the matches in the U.S. While some of the World Cup matches will take place in Canada, which is more lenient with visa policies, the absence of Ivorian fans from the U.S. matches will undoubtedly affect the team’s experience at the World Cup.
Côte d’Ivoire’s journey to 2026 is more than just a football story. It’s about a nation coming together to support its team, and that support may be diminished if fans are unable to attend the tournament in full.
Senegal has long been considered one of Africa’s top footballing nations. The Lions of Teranga have qualified for the 2026 World Cup, bringing their world-class talent to the tournament once again. With players like Sadio Mané and Kalidou Koulibaly, Senegal is a team to watch in 2026, and their fans are eager to support them in the tournament. The team has been drawn into a group with France and Norway, among others, and is expected to be one of the top contenders for a deep run.
However, as with Côte d’Ivoire, Haiti, and Iran, Senegalese fans face travel difficulties due to U.S. visa restrictions. While some of Senegal’s games will be played in Canada, the team’s matches in the United States may be out of reach for many supporters. The U.S. travel restrictions on ordinary citizens from Senegal will prevent many of the country’s most dedicated fans from attending. This is a major blow, as the Senegalese fanbase is known for its energy and support.
Senegal’s journey to the World Cup is a source of national pride, and the absence of fans from the stadiums will be felt both by the team and the supporters back home. The emotional connection between the team and its fans is an integral part of the football experience, and this separation will create a sense of loss for many.
For football fans around the world, the FIFA World Cup is an event of unparalleled significance. It is a time when nations unite, when communities come together, and when the passion for the game reaches its peak. For fans of Iran, Haiti, Ivory Coast, and Senegal, the prospect of missing out on the opportunity to witness their teams live in action at the World Cup is devastating.
Football is more than just a game; it is a way of life for millions of people. For these countries, the World Cup is not just about the competition; it is about pride, unity, and hope. It is about celebrating the players who have given everything for their country and watching them take on the best teams in the world. The absence of these supporters from the stands will create a gap that cannot easily be filled.
While Canada provides a partial relief with more lenient visa policies, the United States will be the central hub for much of the action, and it is here where many fans are likely to be denied entry. The emotional toll this takes on fans is hard to quantify. The U.S. travel ban on countries like Iran, Haiti, Côte d’Ivoire, and Senegal sends a stark message to football fans everywhere: the passion for the game should not be restricted by politics.
The FIFA World Cup 2026 is more than just a tournament; it is a global celebration of football, culture, and unity. It brings together countries from all over the world to share in the joy of the game. But for Iranian, Haitian, Ivorian, and Senegalese fans, the celebration will be incomplete. They will be left out of one of the biggest moments in football history, simply because of political barriers that limit their ability to attend.
While the teams may continue to compete, the absence of their most dedicated supporters will be felt across the stadiums. Football without fans is not the same, and the fate of these fans highlights the growing divide between politics and sport in the modern world.
As the world eagerly anticipates the 2026 World Cup, the question remains: will we allow the spirit of the game to transcend politics, or will the barriers that divide us continue to keep fans apart?
For Iran, Haiti, Ivory Coast and Senegal, the FIFA World Cup 2026 represents more than just a chance to compete. It is a symbol of hope, pride, and unity. But for their fans, the journey to the World Cup is marred by the reality of travel restrictions and visa challenges. As these nations fight for glory on the pitch, their most dedicated supporters may be forced to watch from afar, denied the opportunity to experience the tournament’s magic firsthand.
The 2026 World Cup should be a celebration of football and community, but for many, it will be a bittersweet experience. Only time will tell how this situation will evolve, but one thing is clear: the fate of these fans is a reminder that sport should unite, not divide.

title: United States | FIFA World Cup 2026 update: B-1 visa or ESTA allowed for certain match officials traveling to US - BAL Immigration Law
snippet: United States | FIFA World Cup 2026 update: B-1 visa or ESTA allowed for certain match officials traveling to US  BAL Immigration Law
content: Immigration News - Business travel, Work authorization Trump, United States United States | FIFA World Cup 2026 update: B-1 visa or ESTA allowed for certain match officials traveling to US Share this article LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) May 14, 2025 The State Department recently noted a change to the Foreign Affairs Manual (FAM) clarifying that certain match officials can travel to the U.S. on a B-1 business visa or Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA). Key Points: The FAM was “revised to add participants in international sporting events for visa processing” on April 14, 2025, in advance of FIFA World Cup 2026, the 2028 Olympic and Paralympic Games and other sporting events. The policy manual now clarifies that certain referees, judges and technical officials may be issued a B-1 visa if they have been hired to serve in their officiate capacity at a sporting event with an international dimension. Eligible match officials may also use ESTA to facilitate their travel to the U.S. Additional Information: The new policy language falls under 9 FAM 402.2-5 (C)(10) (U) Participants in International Sporting Events (CT:VISA-2145; 04-14-2025) and reads: Referees, Judges and Technical Officials: A referee, judge, or technical official may be issued a B-1 visa if the applicant has been hired subject to a selection process to referee, judge, oversee, or officiate a sporting event with an international dimension (such as FIFA World Cup or the Olympics). Technical officials are responsible for applying the rules or regulations of individual sports to make judgments on performance, ranking, time or rule infringement, and ensure that all athletes can compete fairly (for example, timekeepers, weigh-in officials, starters, lane inspectors, and stroke and turn officials). Such aliens may not receive a salary or payment for services from a U.S. source, other than remuneration for incidental expenses. This alert has been provided by the BAL U.S. Practice Group. title: FIFA World Cup 2026: Canada government issues entry rules. How fans, players and media can apply for visa or eTA | Step-by-step guide - The Indian Express
snippet: FIFA World Cup 2026: Canada government issues entry rules. How fans, players and media can apply for visa or eTA | Step-by-step guide  The Indian Express
content:
The government of Canada has issued fresh guidance for fans, players, media and workers planning to travel for the FIFA World Cup 2026, urging visitors to apply early and ensure they have the correct entry documents.
“There is no special FIFA World Cup visa,” the government states, adding that most visitors will enter Canada “as tourists” and must meet standard immigration requirements.
The FIFA World Cup 2026 will be jointly hosted by sixteen cities, eleven in the United States, three in Mexico, and two in Canada. The tournament will be the first to be hosted by three nations.
Travellers may require either:
What you need depends on your citizenship and how you plan to travel to Canada.
Importantly, having a match ticket does not guarantee approval. “Having a FIFA Cup event ticket doesn’t guarantee that your visa or eTA application will be approved,” the guidance says.
Applicants must apply online through official Canadian immigration channels. Authorities have advised travellers to clearly indicate in their application that they are visiting for the FIFA World Cup, although this is “for tracking purposes only”.
Officials warn applicants to apply well in advance so they “don’t miss your event”.
The guidance applies to anyone travelling to Canada for matches during the FIFA World Cup 2026. Applications should be submitted as early as possible due to expected high demand.
Step-by-step guide
The Government of Canada cautions travellers against immigration scams. “No one can guarantee you a visa or an eTA,” it says. Providing false information could result in a five-year ban from entering Canada.
Travellers are encouraged to use official government sources for accurate information.
